threats.title: Native Token Deposit/Repay/Bid Flows Broken Due to WETH Approval Requirement After Wrap
threats.hypothesis_description: wrapNativeTokenInWallet wraps ETH to WETH and transfers it to the user via IWETH.transferFrom(pool, user). Subsequently, executeDepositERC20/executeRepay/executeAuction calls erc20TransferInLiquidity which does safeTransferFrom(user, pool, amount), requiring the user to have WETH approval for the pool. Users depositing native ETH will not have pre-approved the pool for WETH, causing all native token deposit, repay, and bid transactions to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wrap-then-pull pattern creates a circular dependency: ETH is wrapped to WETH and sent to the user, then immediately pulled back. The pull requires WETH approval that a native ETH user wouldn't have. This breaks a core protocol feature across multiple entry points.

proof_of_signal.pattern: wrapNativeTokenInWallet transfers WETH to user via transferFrom(pool, user). Then erc20TransferInLiquidity in the caller does safeTransferFrom(user, pool, amount) requiring user WETH approval.

preconditions: User calls any native-token function (depositERC20, repay, auction, etc.) with NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS
User has NOT pre-approved pool contract for WETH spending

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: WETH transferFrom(pool, user) followed by safeTransferFrom(user, pool) without user approval
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: In WETH9, transferFrom checks allowance when src != msg.sender. The pool calling safeTransferFrom(user, pool, amount) requires user's WETH allowance for the pool.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No self-approval mechanism, no use of WETH.transfer() instead of transferFrom()

analysis_questions: Does the specific WETH implementation on target chains skip allowance for self-transfers?
Are there any pre-deployment WETH approval steps in the deployment scripts?
Can users work around this by pre-approving WETH?
validation_requirements: Test native ETH deposit on target WETH implementation
Verify revert occurs at safeTransferFrom step
Check all native token code paths affected

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not an attack per se but a complete DoS of native token functionality. Any user attempting to deposit ETH, repay with ETH, or bid with ETH will have their transaction revert.
prerequisites: User interacts with NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS without prior WETH approval

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete inability to use native ETH for deposits, repayments, bids, and liquidations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting native ETH operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users can manually wrap and approve WETH as workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No integration test for native ETH deposit/repay flows with actual WETH contract

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately detectable on first native token operation

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Bypasses availableLiquidity Tracking and Can Access Bid Escrow Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan performs raw safeTransfer without checking assetData.availableLiquidity. The flash loan amount is not validated against available liquidity - it can access the entire token balance including bid escrow amounts (totalBidAmout). This can temporarily disrupt auction bid refunds during the callback and violates the solvency invariant that bid amounts are always available for refund.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pool maintains separate accounting for availableLiquidity and totalBidAmout. Bid escrow must always be available for refund to outbid bidders. Flash loans bypassing this accounting can temporarily make bid refunds impossible during callback, enabling griefing of auction participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does IERC20.safeTransfer without checking availableLiquidity or totalBidAmout; no validation that amount <= availableLiquidity

preconditions: Flash loan enabled for the asset
Pool holds tokens from both liquidity and bid escrow
Flash loan amount > availableLiquidity but <= total balance

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Can flash loan bid escrow funds. During callback, erc20TransferOutBidAmount would fail for auction refunds.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(amount <= assetData.availableLiquidity) before flash loan transfer

analysis_questions: Can this be combined with reentrancy to disrupt ongoing auctions?
What operations during callback rely on actual token balance vs tracked liquidity?
Can the attacker profit from temporarily disrupting bid refunds?
validation_requirements: Flash loan amount exceeding availableLiquidity but within total balance
Verify bid refund failure during callback
Test auction bidding during flash loan callback

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Pool has 100 WETH liquidity + 50 WETH bid escrow = 150 WETH balance. 2) Attacker flash loans 140 WETH (exceeds availableLiquidity but pool has balance). 3) During callback, any bid operation requiring outbid refund fails. 4) Tokens returned after callback.
prerequisites: Active auction bids in escrow for same token

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary disruption of auction refunds; potential griefing of auction participants

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for flash loan accessing bid escrow amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Flash loan amount visible in events

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Missing Balance Delta Verification Enables Token Drain with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan perform raw safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom without pre/post balance checks. Unlike erc20TransferInLiquidity and erc20TransferOutLiquidity which verify balance deltas, flash loan transfers do not validate that the exact amount was received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the pool loses value on each flash loan (fee deducted on both outgoing and incoming transfer).
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states support for fee-on-transfer tokens. Other transfer functions in VaultLogic have balance delta checks, but flash loan transfers do not. This creates a slow drain vector through repeated flash loans.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is configured as a pool asset with flash loan enabled
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states 'ERC20 (Any implementation, explicitly including weird traits: fee-on-transfer)'. No deployment evidence of specific fee-on-transfer tokens configured.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan lack pre/post balance checks present in erc20TransferInLiquidity (lines 300-310)

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured as pool asset
Flash loan is enabled for that token
Token charges fee on both transfer directions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No balanceOf checks before/after transfers unlike other transfer functions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For 1% fee-on-transfer: flash loan 1000, receiver gets 990, returns 1000 but pool receives 990. Pool loses 10 per cycle.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing pre/post balance checks, missing require(poolSizeAfter >= poolSizeBefore)

analysis_questions: Which configured tokens have fee-on-transfer behavior?
How many flash loan cycles to drain significant liquidity?
Does the pool's tracked availableLiquidity become inconsistent with actual balance?
validation_requirements: Test with fee-on-transfer token mock
Verify pool balance decreases after flash loan round-trip
Calculate drain rate for realistic fee percentages

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Pool has 10000 FEE_TOKEN (1% transfer fee). 2) Attacker flash loans 10000. Pool sends 10000, attacker receives 9900. 3) Attacker approves and returns 10000. Pool receives 9900. 4) Pool lost 200 tokens total (2 * 1% * 10000). 5) Repeat.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gradual drain of fee-on-transfer token liquidity through repeated flash loans
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires disabling flash loan for affected tokens

threats.test_coverage_gap: No flash loan test with fee-on-transfer tokens

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be repeated rapidly in multiple transactions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Pool balance decreasing without corresponding state changes

threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Passes Inflated liquidityAdded to updateInterestRates
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, updateInterestRates is called with liquidityAdded = (vars.totalBorrowAmount + vars.totalExtraAmount). However, totalBorrowAmount is repaid from bid escrow (erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity moves from totalBidAmout to availableLiquidity), and only totalExtraAmount is new external liquidity. The interest rate calculation sees inflated liquidity addition, computing lower utilization and rates than warranted.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rate calculation formula is: availableLiquidityPlusDebt = availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded - liquidityTaken + totalDebt. If liquidityAdded is double-counted (bid escrow already in pool balance + counted as new liquidity), utilization drops artificially, setting lower rates for all borrowers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateInterestRates(poolData, debtAssetData, (vars.totalBorrowAmount + vars.totalExtraAmount), 0). But erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity already increased availableLiquidity by totalBorrowAmount.

preconditions: Isolate auction has ended
Liquidation is executed
Significant bid amounts relative to pool size

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: liquidityAdded includes totalBorrowAmount from bid escrow which already increased availableLiquidity via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Double-counting: bid escrow moves to availableLiquidity (increasing it), then updateInterestRates adds it again as liquidityAdded
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should pass only totalExtraAmount as liquidityAdded, not totalBorrowAmount + totalExtraAmount

analysis_questions: How significant is the rate impact for typical liquidation sizes?
Does this create persistent rate miscalculation or just transient?
Can an attacker strategically trigger liquidations to manipulate rates?
validation_requirements: Trace the exact liquidity flow during isolate liquidation
Calculate rate difference with correct vs inflated liquidityAdded
Verify if availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded is double-counted

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Large isolate liquidation with 100 ETH bid amount. erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity increases availableLiquidity by 100. updateInterestRates gets liquidityAdded=100+extra, seeing 200 ETH of new liquidity instead of just extra. Utilization drops, rates decrease artificially.
prerequisites: Isolate auction ending with significant bid amounts

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Artificially suppressed interest rates after liquidations, reducing lender yield
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders of the affected asset
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Rates corrected on next operation, but damage in that block is permanent

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying correct interest rate update after isolate liquidation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Rate changes visible but may appear normal

threats.title: Native ERC20 withdrawal path unwraps using the original requested amount (not the capped/actual withdrawn amount), causing reverts or unintended extra WETH pull from msgSender
threats.hypothesis_description: BVault.withdrawERC20 passes the user-supplied `amount` into unwrapNativeTokenInWallet after SupplyLogic.executeWithdrawERC20 may have reduced params.amount to the user's actual balance. Because the struct is passed by value, BVault does not learn the capped amount. unwrapNativeTokenInWallet then transferFroms WETH from msgSender for the (too-large) original amount, reverting or potentially pulling additional WETH from msgSender if they have balance+allowance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A identified this as an invariant failure (INV-STATE-002). This is a concrete code defect in a frequently-used path (withdraw all). It can brick native withdrawals and may cause unexpected token movement depending on user allowances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: after executeWithdrawERC20(...), calls unwrapNativeTokenInWallet(asset, msgSender, amount) using original amount variable

preconditions: User calls withdrawERC20 with asset == NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS
User requests amount > actual withdrawable (common 'withdraw all' patterns)
unwrapNativeTokenInWallet uses transferFrom(user->pool), requiring allowance and sufficient balance

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: BVault.withdrawERC20() native unwrap after library call
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: stale parameter used after internal capping in a memory struct
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: native withdrawal reverts or may pull extra WETH from msgSender beyond what was withdrawn
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No return value from executeWithdrawERC20 with actual amount; no receiver==msgSender enforcement for native path

analysis_questions: Can receiver differ from msgSender in native path, causing unwrap to pull WETH from msgSender while WETH was sent to receiver?
Is there any recommended UI pattern to pass type(uint256).max (withdraw all) that will systematically revert?
Could an attacker exploit user allowances to cause extra WETH to be pulled during native withdraw?
validation_requirements: PoC: supply 5 WETH, withdraw native with amount=10 or max; observe revert
PoC: receiver != msgSender; observe mismatch between withdrawn WETH recipient and unwrap payer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User tries to withdraw all as native ETH using amount=max. Withdrawal internally caps but unwrap still tries to move max WETH from user, reverting and preventing withdrawal.
prerequisites: User has supplied WETH and uses native withdrawal flag

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational DoS for native withdrawals; potential unintended additional WETH pull if user has allowance+balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All WETH suppliers using native withdrawal option
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High via patch; but users impacted until upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for withdraw-all (amount > balance) and receiver != msgSender in native mode

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track revert reason ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED / TOKEN_TRANSFER_FAILED on native withdrawals

threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Sends NFT to Caller Instead of Winning Bidder - Free NFT Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, the NFT collateral is transferred to params.msgSender (the caller of isolateLiquidate) instead of loanData.lastBidder (the auction winner). The winning bidder's escrowed funds are consumed to repay the debt, but the NFT goes to whoever calls the function after the auction ends. Any address can call isolateLiquidate—there is no access control restricting it to the winning bidder.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code at IsolateLogic.sol:370-377 unconditionally transfers the NFT to params.msgSender. The bidder's bid amount (stored in loanData.bidAmount) is moved from escrow to repay debt via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity. The winning bidder loses their entire bid and receives nothing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) and VaultLogic.erc721TransferOutLiquidity(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) use params.msgSender instead of loanData.lastBidder

preconditions: An isolate loan auction has ended (block.timestamp > bidStartTimestamp + auctionDuration)
loanData.lastBidder != address(0) and loanData.bidAmount > 0
Attacker has a different address than loanData.lastBidder

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: NFT recipient is params.msgSender at line 373/377, not loanData.lastBidder
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker monitors on-chain for auctions past auctionEndTimestamp, then calls isolateLiquidate before the legitimate bidder
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives NFT valued >= bidAmount for free (or only paying totalExtraAmount if bid < debt)

economic_questions: What is the expected NFT value relative to bid amounts in active auctions?
Does the attacker need to pay any extraBorrowAmount if bid fully covers debt?
Can the legitimate bidder front-run the attacker?
validation_requirements: Confirm no access control restricting isolateLiquidate caller to lastBidder
Confirm extraBorrowAmounts[nidx] == 0 when bidAmount >= borrowAmount
Test end-to-end: third party calls isolateLiquidate and receives NFT

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 ETH if bidAmount >= borrowAmount; only extraAmount otherwise
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of the NFT collateral (potentially tens of ETH per NFT)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI when no extra payment required

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None—attacker needs no capital if bid covers debt
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct theft from auction bidders; undermines entire auction mechanism

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of entire bid amount + failure to receive NFT

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Auction duration is 1 day per config; NFT floor prices range from single-digit to hundreds of ETH

threats.title: Isolate Auction Interest Accrual Between Bid and Liquidation Can Create Underpaid Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: Interest accrues between the time a bid is placed and when liquidation is executed. The bid amount is fixed at bidding time, but borrowAmount grows with compound interest. In executeIsolateLiquidate, if bidAmount < borrowAmount, the difference (extraBorrowAmounts) must be paid by the caller. However, the caller is NOT the bidder (per ECON-H001). Combined with the NFT theft issue, an attacker can time liquidation to maximize the interval, ensuring extraBorrowAmounts pushes cost onto the legitimate bidder if they try to call liquidate.
threats.investigation_rationale: IsolateLogic.sol executeIsolateLiquidate lines 340-350: borrowAmount = loanData.scaledAmount.rayMul(vars.normalizedIndex) grows over time. loanData.bidAmount is fixed. The gap extraBorrowAmounts = borrowAmount - bidAmount increases with time. The auction duration may be configured to 1 day, during which significant interest can accrue on high-rate loans.

proof_of_signal.pattern: borrowAmount computed with current normalizedIndex; bidAmount is fixed from auction time; gap paid by params.msgSender

preconditions: Active isolate auction with high interest rate group
Significant time between last bid and auction end
Interest accrues enough that bidAmount < borrowAmount at liquidation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: borrowAmount at liquidation = scaledAmount * currentBorrowIndex > bidAmount at bid time
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Bidder locks in bid amount; interest growth creates gap; liquidation caller must cover difference
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: At 100% APY over 1 day auction, interest accrual ≈ 0.27% of principal; for large loans this is material

economic_questions: What is the maximum interest accrual over the auction duration for each rate tier?
Can a bidder bid the minimum amount knowing interest will create a gap?
Combined with ECON-H001, can this be exploited to force costs onto the NFT thief?
validation_requirements: Calculate interest accrual for 1-day auction with high rate group (100% APY)
Verify extraBorrowAmounts computation is correct
Test end-to-end with time progression between bid and liquidation

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 0.1-0.3% of loan value per day in interest gap
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Low per event; systemic impact on auction economics

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High utilization periods with elevated borrow rates

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: auctionDuration = 1 day; borrow rates 5-100% APY; typical loan sizes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare borrowAmount at bid time vs liquidation time

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 Remainder Credited as Supply Without Interest Rate Update for Collateral
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCrossLiquidateERC721, when remainDebtToLiquidate > 0 (NFT collateral value exceeds total debt), the excess is credited to the borrower via erc20IncreaseCrossSupply for the debt asset. However, no interest rate update is called for the debt asset after this supply increase—the rate update only accounts for the actualDebtToLiquidate as liquidityAdded, not the remainder that effectively becomes new supply. This causes a mismatch between tracked supply and the rates computed.
threats.investigation_rationale: LiquidationLogic.sol executeCrossLiquidateERC721 lines 190-196: remainDebtToLiquidate > 0 causes erc20IncreaseCrossSupply(debtAssetData, borrower, remainDebtToLiquidate). But updateInterestRates at line 200 only passes actualDebtToLiquidate as liquidityAdded. The remainder supply increase is not reflected in rate calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20IncreaseCrossSupply for remainDebtToLiquidate occurs after updateInterestRates which does not account for it

preconditions: ERC721 cross-liquidation where collateral value significantly exceeds total debt
remainDebtToLiquidate > 0 after repaying all group debts
Non-trivial remainder amount

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: updateInterestRates uses actualDebtToLiquidate but not remainDebtToLiquidate
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Repeated ERC721 liquidations with large remainders accumulate supply without rate adjustment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Interest rates become stale relative to actual supply/demand, benefiting borrowers

economic_questions: How large can remainDebtToLiquidate be relative to total supply?
Does the rate mismatch compound across multiple liquidation events?
Does the next operation on the asset correct the rates?
validation_requirements: Calculate typical remainder amounts for ERC721 liquidations
Compare rates before and after liquidation with large remainder
Verify whether next deposit/borrow corrects the rate discrepancy

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Capital to cover debt for ERC721 liquidation
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Minor rate inaccuracy per liquidation event

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient to cover ERC721 liquidation debt
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When NFT collateral significantly exceeds debt value

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Interest income deviation for the affected period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: NFT prices vs typical borrow amounts; liquidationBonus = 1000 (10%)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute rate deviation from missing remainder in liquidityAdded

threats.title: Underflow in totalBidAmout during isolate liquidation permanently blocks liquidations
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, the protocol subtracts totalBorrowAmount from assetData.totalBidAmout. However, the amount originally added to totalBidAmout during the auction was loanData.bidAmount. Since totalBorrowAmount includes accrued interest and can exceed bidAmount, this subtraction will underflow and revert if there are no other bids in escrow, permanently blocking the liquidation.
threats.investigation_rationale: This directly breaks the liquidation mechanism for isolated NFTs, leaving the protocol with unrecoverable bad debt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assetData.totalBidAmout -= amount; where amount is vars.totalBorrowAmount

preconditions: Isolate loan is in auction
Accrued interest causes borrowAmount to exceed bidAmount
No other active auctions hold sufficient escrow to cover the difference

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol suffers permanent bad debt as liquidations revert

economic_questions: Can the underflow be reliably triggered by waiting for interest to accrue?
Does it steal from other users' escrows if they exist?
validation_requirements: Simulate an auction where borrowAmount grows larger than bidAmount before liquidation

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Protocol loss equal to loan value

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After auction ends and interest accrues

threats.title: Arbitrary onBehalf parameter in isolate repay allows targeted debt reduction or DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: validateIsolateRepayBasic does not verify that onBehalf matches the NFT owner. An attacker can repay a loan specifying an arbitrary onBehalf address, causing that address's userScaledIsolateBorrow to decrease while the actual borrower's debt remains artificially inflated. This can be used to grief users by causing underflows in their future repayments.
threats.investigation_rationale: Allows arbitrary manipulation of user debt accounting, leading to DoS of legitimate repayments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.onBehalf, vars.scaledRepayAmount);

preconditions: Victim has an active isolate loan
Attacker calls isolateRepay with victim's NFT but a different onBehalf address

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker repays loan but credits debt reduction to another user
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack causing permanent DoS for the targeted user's future repayments due to underflow

economic_questions: Can this be used to extract value, or only grief?
Does it permanently brick the victim's account?
validation_requirements: Test repaying with mismatched onBehalf and verify underflow on subsequent actions

threats.title: Original borrowers retain NFT delegation rights after liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: executeDelegateERC721 allows borrowers to delegate NFT utility rights via DelegateRegistryV2. If the NFT is liquidated with supplyAsCollateral = true, the protocol retains ownership but transfers internal accounting to the liquidator. However, the external delegation is never revoked, allowing the original borrower to permanently retain utility/airdrop rights at the liquidator's expense.
threats.investigation_rationale: Liquidators assume they are acquiring full rights to the NFT, but the original owner secretly retains utility value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing delegation revocation when transferring internal ownership to liquidator

preconditions: Borrower delegates NFT via executeDelegateERC721
NFT is liquidated with supplyAsCollateral = true

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Delegation state in external registry outlives internal ownership transfer
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Borrower intentionally gets liquidated while retaining delegation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Borrower extracts financial value from the loan AND retains airdrop/utility value of the NFT

economic_questions: Can the liquidator overwrite the delegation?
Does DelegateRegistryV2 clear delegations on internal accounting changes?
validation_requirements: Verify delegation persists after liquidation and liquidator cannot easily revoke it

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Utility value of all delegated NFTs

threats.title: Malicious bidders can permanently lock isolate auctions using reverting fallbacks or blocklists
threats.hypothesis_description: executeIsolateAuction refunds the previous bidder using erc20TransferOutBidAmount. If the previous bidder is a smart contract that reverts on ERC20 transfers (for tokens with callbacks) or an address blacklisted by the token (e.g., USDC), the refund will revert. This prevents anyone from outbidding them, allowing the attacker to win the auction at the minimum price.
threats.investigation_rationale: Push-based refunds are a known anti-pattern. Attackers can exploit this to steal NFTs at a massive discount.
deployment_assumptions: Pool supports USDC or ERC777 tokens as debt assets
threats.config_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol adds USDC to the common pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc20TransferOutBidAmount(debtAssetData, vars.oldLastBidder, vars.oldBidAmount);

preconditions: Debt asset has a blocklist (USDC) or transfer callbacks
Attacker places a bid and then triggers the revert condition (e.g., gets blacklisted)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Push-based refund inside the bidding function
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker forces refund to revert, blocking new bids
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker wins the NFT for the minimum bid, extracting the difference between bid and market value

economic_questions: Is it feasible to get blacklisted on demand?
Are there any ERC777 tokens supported?
validation_requirements: Simulate a reverting transfer in the refund path and verify auction is locked

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI depending on NFT value

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of collateral value for protocol

threats.title: Fee Collection Order-of-Operations Bug - Treasury Receives Slightly Inflated Supply Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCollectFeeToTreasury, the accruedFee is read and zeroed BEFORE calling updateInterestIndexs. Then updateInterestIndexs updates the supply index and accrues new fees. The old accruedFee is then multiplied by the NEW (higher) normalizedIncome to determine amountToCollect. This means the treasury receives supply shares calculated at the updated index for fees that were accrued at the old index, resulting in slightly more shares than warranted. The difference is extracted from existing suppliers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code at PoolLogic.sol reads accruedFee, zeros it, then calls updateInterestIndexs which modifies supplyIndex. The subsequent rayMul with getNormalizedSupplyIncome uses the new index. The fee was accumulated as accruedFee += amount.rayDiv(oldSupplyIndex), but is collected as accruedFee.rayMul(newSupplyIndex), so if supplyIndex increased between accumulation and collection, the treasury gets a windfall.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accruedFee read and zeroed at line ~50, updateInterestIndexs called at line ~55, normalizedIncome computed at line ~56 using updated index

preconditions: Fee factor > 0 for the asset
Time has passed since last index update (supply rate > 0)
Pool admin calls collectFeeToTreasury

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: accruedFee zeroed before index update, then old fee multiplied by new index
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Triggered by pool admin but the bug is in the code logic
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every fee collection call when time has elapsed since last update

operational_questions: What is the magnitude of the excess shares per collection?
Over many collections, does the cumulative excess amount become significant?
Could admin strategically time collections to maximize the discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Compare fee collected with correct ordering vs current ordering
Quantify the excess per collection as a function of time elapsed and supply rate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: This is a systemic accounting error, not a direct attack vector
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Admin could maximize the effect by calling collectFeeToTreasury after long periods without any pool activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The longer since last index update, the larger the index jump and the larger the treasury windfall
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Pool admin access required to trigger collection

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Slow dilution of supplier positions; treasury receives more shares than earned
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix; past excess shares cannot be easily reversed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Fee collection mechanism; affects all suppliers' share value

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None for the bug; pool admin role for triggering
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: More impactful after longer periods without interaction

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare collected fee amounts vs expected based on borrow activity
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Treasury share balance growing faster than fee accrual rate

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 - Incorrect collateralItemDebtToCover Division Creates Rounding Exploit for Multi-NFT Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calculateDebtAmountFromERC721Collateral, the per-item debt coverage is calculated as collateralTotalDebtToCover / userCollateralBalance (integer division). When the result is small or has significant rounding loss, the liquidation price per NFT may be significantly lower than the fair proportion. With multiple NFTs being liquidated, the total debtAmountNeeded (which uses the per-item liquidate price × number of tokens) can underpay the actual proportional debt, allowing the liquidator to acquire NFTs for less than their fair debt coverage.
threats.investigation_rationale: The division collateralTotalDebtToCover / liqVars.userCollateralBalance rounds down in Solidity. For small debt-to-collateral ratios or large userCollateralBalance, this truncation loses significant value. The liquidator pays debtAmountNeeded which is based on the truncated per-item value multiplied by the number of tokens being liquidated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: vars.collateralItemDebtToCover = vars.collateralTotalDebtToCover / liqVars.userCollateralBalance; integer division truncation amplified by multiplication

preconditions: User has multiple ERC721 collateral with cross-margin borrows
Health factor < 1 triggering liquidation eligibility
User's debt-to-collateral ratio creates significant rounding in per-item division

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division of total debt by total NFT count, then multiplication back by liquidated count

operational_questions: What is the maximum rounding loss per NFT in base currency terms?
Can an attacker structure their position to maximize the rounding benefit?
Does the remainDebtToLiquidate correctly handle the discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Calculate concrete rounding loss examples with real NFT collection sizes
Test multi-NFT liquidation with edge case debt amounts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit many NFTs as cross collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Borrow amount designed to create maximum per-item rounding loss
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Liquidator liquidates subset of NFTs paying less than proportional debt

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Underpayment on liquidations; bad debt accumulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cumulative rounding losses are not recoverable

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - precise calculation of optimal position
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When NFT prices drop below liquidation threshold

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC721 liquidations where debtAmountNeeded < expected proportional debt
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated positions with unusual NFT counts and debt amounts

threats.title: Supply/borrow index uint128 casting may brick assets under extreme rate model outputs or long inactivity windows
threats.hypothesis_description: InterestLogic stores supplyIndex and borrowIndex as uint128 and uses SafeCastUpgradeable.toUint128() when updating. Under extreme borrowRate/supplyRate values (e.g., misconfigured/buggy interest rate model) or very long time deltas, next indexes can exceed uint128 max, causing permanent reverts on updateInterestIndexs for the asset, effectively bricking all operations touching that asset.
threats.investigation_rationale: While reaching uint128 max requires very large growth from 1e27, the rate model is an external contract per group (GroupData.rateModel) and can be upgraded/configured. A single misconfiguration can cause non-recoverable DoS for that asset without a migration path shown here.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures an interest rate model that can return extremely high rates or behaves unexpectedly
Asset experiences long periods without interactions, increasing (block.timestamp - lastUpdateTimestamp)
threats.config_evidence: GroupData.rateModel is externally set via configuration (Configurator not shown). InterestLogic.updateInterestRates calls rateModel.calculateGroupBorrowRate and stores to uint128.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nextSupplyIndex/nextBorrowIndex cast via toUint128(); overflow reverts and blocks further operations

preconditions: borrowRate/supplyRate becomes very large OR time delta becomes very large
An operation triggers updateInterestIndexs for the asset
No administrative recovery mechanism exists to reset indexes safely

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Narrowing cast of critical accumulator state
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Permanent asset-level DoS and protocol insolvency risk if users cannot repay/withdraw
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No upper-bound checks or circuit-breakers on rate outputs/time delta before casting

analysis_questions: What are realistic maximum outputs from DefaultInterestRateModel and can they be admin-upgraded?
Is there any admin function to pause an asset or change rateModel to recover, given updates still revert?
Could a malicious rateModel be installed by a compromised admin (operational) or via config bug?
validation_requirements: Compute worst-case index growth under max expected rates and inactivity windows
Unit tests with mocked IRM returning extreme rates to see if/when cast reverts
Review configurator/admin controls for rateModel upgrades and emergency remediation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Through governance misconfiguration or a buggy IRM upgrade, borrowRate spikes and nextBorrowIndex exceeds uint128 max. Subsequent user actions (repay/liquidate/withdraw) revert when calling updateInterestIndexs, freezing the market and potentially locking funds.
prerequisites: Misconfigured or buggy interest rate model, or extreme time delta

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Market freeze and inability to repay/withdraw, potentially leading to cascading insolvency
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the affected asset across the pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Likely requires upgrade and state migration; difficult if all state-changing paths revert

threats.test_coverage_gap: No stress tests for index overflow boundaries

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor index values approaching uint128 max and abnormal rate outputs

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Reentrancy Enables Borrowing at Manipulated Interest Rates
threats.hypothesis_description: flashLoanERC20 lacks nonReentrant modifier. During the IFlashLoanReceiver callback, availableLiquidity is NOT decremented for flash-loaned tokens. An attacker can re-enter executeCrossBorrowERC20 during the callback. The borrow's updateInterestRates call sees inflated availableLiquidity, computing artificially low utilization and therefore lower borrow rates. The actual token transfer for the borrow succeeds if pool retains sufficient unlent balance. The attacker locks in below-market borrow rates that persist until the next rate update on that asset.
threats.investigation_rationale: The flash loan deliberately omits nonReentrant to allow composability, but this creates a window where availableLiquidity is stale. Interest rate manipulation during this window directly impacts all depositors' yield via suppressed supply rates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: flashLoanERC20 has whenNotPaused but NO nonReentrant modifier; FlashLoanLogic transfers tokens out without updating availableLiquidity, then invokes external callback

preconditions: Flash loans enabled for target ERC20 asset
Pool has sufficient pre-existing liquidity beyond the flash loan amount to fund the reentrant borrow
Attacker has collateral deposited to pass borrow validation
No external reentrancy guard in PoolManager dispatch preventing re-entry to CrossLending module

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: updateInterestRates uses assetData.availableLiquidity which is not decremented during flash loan; utilization = totalDebt / (availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded - liquidityTaken + totalDebt)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan 80% of pool liquidity. During callback, availableLiquidity appears 5x higher than actual. Utilization drops from e.g. 65% to 13%, reducing borrow rate by ~75%
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker borrows at artificially suppressed rate. Rate persists until next interaction. For large borrows on assets with infrequent interaction, interest savings can be substantial.

economic_questions: Does the PoolManager dispatch allow re-entry from flash loan callback to CrossLending module?
What is the maximum rate suppression achievable given typical pool utilization?
How long do manipulated rates persist on average before next interaction?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: flash loan → callback → crossBorrowERC20 → verify rate recorded is below market
Measure actual rate difference between normal borrow and flash-loan-reentrant borrow
Verify PoolManager dispatch does not independently block reentrancy

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Collateral for borrow only; flash loan is free (no fee)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on borrow size and rate suppression duration; potentially significant for large borrows
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Interest rate savings proportional to utilization manipulation (requires validation)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block atomic execution; rate effect persists until next asset interaction

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool must have enough unlent liquidity for both flash loan and subsequent borrow
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool has high utilization (maximizing rate difference) and low interaction frequency
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Suppressed rates reduce supplier yield; protocol earns less fees

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All suppliers of the affected asset
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced yield proportional to rate suppression period
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Interest income for entire pool during rate suppression window

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Flash loan amounts vs pool liquidity ratios for deployed pools
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute utilization with and without flash loan to quantify rate delta

threats.title: IsolateRedeem Decreases Wrong User's Borrow Balance Enabling Debt Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: executeIsolateRedeem calls erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled) using the caller's address instead of the actual loan borrower (tokenData.owner). An attacker with their own isolate borrow in the same group can call isolateRedeem on another user's auctioned loan, getting the attacker's own borrow balance decreased while paying the redeem amount. The loan's scaledAmount is correctly decreased but per-user accounting is corrupted, breaking the invariant that totalScaledIsolateBorrow == sum(userScaledIsolateBorrow).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect where the wrong address is used for a critical accounting operation. The bid fine paid to firstBidder via erc20TransferBetweenWallets adds additional cost, but the accounting corruption has systemic consequences beyond direct profit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled) uses params.msgSender instead of tokenData.owner

preconditions: Target loan must be in LOAN_STATUS_AUCTION
Attacker must have their own isolate borrow in the same debt asset and group with userScaledIsolateBorrow >= vars.amountScaled
Auction duration has not ended (block.timestamp <= auctionEndTimestamp)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow targets params.msgSender, reducing attacker's borrow by redeemAmount while original borrower's userScaledIsolateBorrow is unchanged
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls isolateRedeem on another user's auctioned NFT. Pays redeemAmount + bidFine. Gets own borrow reduced by redeemAmount. Net cost is only the bidFine.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If bidFine < interest savings from reduced borrow, attacker profits. Additionally, original borrower's inflated userScaledIsolateBorrow creates phantom debt preventing proper repayment.

economic_questions: Is the bid fine always less than the interest savings from borrow reduction?
What happens to the original borrower's accounting - can they ever fully repay?
Can this be chained to create unbounded accounting corruption?
validation_requirements: PoC: two users with isolate borrows, one in auction, other calls redeem
Verify borrow balance decrease targets caller not borrower
Check if original borrower can still repay after corruption

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient collateral for own isolate borrow + redeemAmount + bidFine
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: redeemAmount - bidFine in effective debt reduction (requires validation)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable; depends on redeemThreshold vs bidFineFactor ratio

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker needs own isolate borrow position as prerequisite
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When target loan enters auction and before auction ends
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Systemic accounting corruption; totalScaledIsolateBorrow drifts from sum of user balances

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Original borrower whose accounting is corrupted; all pool lenders via incorrect interest accrual
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Original borrower may be unable to fully repay their loans
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative phantom borrow amounts across all corrupted positions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: redeemThreshold, bidFineFactor, minBidFineFactor from deployment config
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare attacker's borrow before/after redeem vs original borrower's borrow

threats.title: ERC721 Flash Loan Bypasses lockerAddr Check Allowing Extraction of Yield-Locked NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFlashLoanERC721 checks tokenData.owner and loanData.loanStatus but does NOT check tokenData.lockerAddr. An NFT locked by a yield manager (lockerAddr != address(0)) can be flash-loaned out of the pool. During the callback, the attacker has physical custody of an NFT that the yield protocol believes is locked, enabling manipulation such as claiming airdrops, manipulating delegation, or breaking yield protocol assumptions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lockerAddr mechanism exists specifically to prevent NFT removal while locked for yield. Flash loans bypass this check entirely, undermining the security model of the yield integration. erc721SetTokenLockerAddr sets the lock but flash loans ignore it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Flash loan ERC721 validation checks tokenData.owner and loanData.loanStatus but has no check for tokenData.lockerAddr != address(0)

preconditions: NFT is deposited in pool with lockerAddr set (locked by yield manager)
Flash loans enabled for the NFT asset
Caller is the token owner (tokenData.owner == msgSender)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No lockerAddr check in flash loan validation path
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: NFT owner flash loans their locked NFT, gaining temporary physical custody to claim airdrops, change delegation, or interfere with yield protocol state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Value of claimed airdrops or manipulated yield positions during flash loan window

economic_questions: What yield protocol operations assume the NFT cannot leave the pool?
Are there pending airdrops or rewards claimable by NFT holder?
Can the yield manager's state become inconsistent if NFT briefly leaves?
validation_requirements: Verify flash loan of locked NFT succeeds (no lockerAddr check)
Test yield protocol behavior when locked NFT is temporarily removed
Check if airdrop claims or delegation changes are possible during callback

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: No capital needed beyond gas (flash loan is free)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of claimable airdrops or yield manipulation (variable)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially infinite ROI if valuable airdrop claimable

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When valuable airdrops or delegated actions are available for the NFT

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lost airdrops or corrupted yield state

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Deployed yield managers and their lock assumptions
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Flash loan locked NFT and verify no revert

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 Missing Close Factor Enables Over-Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: executeCrossLiquidateERC20 enforces a close factor (50% when HF close to 1, 100% for deeply underwater positions) via _calculateUserERC20Debt. However, executeCrossLiquidateERC721 has NO close factor mechanism. A liquidator can specify up to MAX_LIQUIDATION_ERC721_TOKEN_NUM tokens to liquidate regardless of how slightly underwater the position is. For a borrower barely below HF=1, the liquidator can seize all their NFTs (capturing the liquidation bonus on each), far exceeding what would be needed to restore solvency.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ERC20 close factor exists to protect borrowers from excessive liquidation. Its absence in the ERC721 path creates an asymmetry that allows liquidators to extract maximum bonus from slightly underwater positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeCrossLiquidateERC721 has no close factor check; compare with _calculateUserERC20Debt lines which enforce closeFactor = DEFAULT_LIQUIDATION_CLOSE_FACTOR or MAX_LIQUIDATION_CLOSE_FACTOR

preconditions: Borrower's health factor is below 1 but close to it (e.g., 0.95-0.99)
Borrower has multiple ERC721 tokens as cross collateral
Liquidation bonus configured for the NFT asset

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _calculateDebtAmountFromERC721Collateral computes debtAmountNeeded = collateralLiquidatePrice * tokenCount; no close factor limits tokenCount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Liquidator specifies all borrower's NFT tokenIds. Pays debt covering all NFTs. Captures liquidation bonus on each NFT.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: With 10% liquidation bonus (configured as 1000 in deployment): liquidator profit = numNFTs * nftPrice * 10%. For 5 NFTs at 10 ETH each: 5 ETH profit instead of ~0.5 ETH with proper close factor.

economic_questions: What is the maximum over-liquidation ratio possible vs ERC20 close factor?
How much excess collateral value is seized vs what's needed to restore HF to 1?
Does the remainDebtToLiquidate credit to borrower adequately compensate?
validation_requirements: Compare ERC20 vs ERC721 liquidation amounts for same HF level
Calculate borrower's loss from full NFT liquidation vs close-factor-limited liquidation
Verify MAX_LIQUIDATION_ERC721_TOKEN_NUM from Constants

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Debt tokens to cover liquidation (returned via collateral bonus)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 10% of total NFT collateral value (vs 10% of close-factor-limited portion)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially 2-10x more than intended liquidation profit

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Liquidator needs debt tokens equal to total NFT liquidation value
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When borrower's HF is just below 1 (maximum over-liquidation ratio)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Borrower loses all NFT collateral when only partial liquidation was needed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Borrowers with ERC721 collateral in cross margin
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of all NFT collateral instead of minimal liquidation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: liquidationBonus per NFT asset from InitConfigPool (1000 = 10%)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate liquidation at various HF levels, compare ERC20 vs ERC721 outcomes

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Has Zero Fee Enabling Free Capital for Atomic Exploits
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFlashLoanERC20 transfers tokens out and requires the same amount returned, but charges zero fee. The flash loan validation (validateFlashLoanERC20Basic) checks only basic params and asset type. Combined with the reentrancy in flashLoanERC20, an attacker gets free capital for any atomic strategy: oracle manipulation, liquidation arbitrage, or cross-protocol exploitation. The absence of fees means there is no economic friction deterring flash loan abuse.
threats.investigation_rationale: Most lending protocols charge flash loan fees (e.g., Aave charges 0.09%). Zero-fee flash loans reduce the cost of every atomic exploit to only gas costs, making borderline-profitable attacks viable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan sends exact amounts; erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan pulls back exact same amounts; no premium/fee calculation or enforcement

preconditions: Flash loans enabled for target asset (assetData.isFlashLoanEnabled)
Sufficient available liquidity in the pool
Attacker has a profitable atomic strategy requiring capital

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan(receiverAddress, assets, amounts) - same amounts as transfer out, zero premium
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Free flash loans enable zero-cost capital for any atomic exploit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All flash-loan-dependent attacks become viable at zero marginal cost

economic_questions: Was the zero fee intentional design or oversight?
What is the maximum flash loanable amount per asset?
Combined with reentrancy, what specific exploits become profitable at zero fee?
validation_requirements: Verify no fee is charged by executing a flash loan and checking balances
Calculate profit thresholds for various exploit strategies with zero vs typical fees
Check if fee-on-transfer tokens could result in loss to pool on return

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable; any atomic strategy profiting more than gas costs is viable
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on combined strategy; zero fee removes capital cost barrier

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool's available liquidity determines maximum flash loan size
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Combined with reentrancy window for maximum impact
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Enables any flash-loan-dependent attack at minimal cost

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Suppliers (no fee income from flash loans); protocol (no revenue)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on downstream exploits enabled by free capital

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Per-asset available liquidity for max flash loan sizing
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Execute flash loan, verify zero premium collected

threats.title: rayMul/rayDiv Rounding Asymmetry Can Prevent Full Debt Repayment in Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _repayUserERC20Debt and executeCrossRepayERC20, the debt amount is computed as scaledBorrow.rayMul(borrowIndex), then repayment divides back: amount.rayDiv(borrowIndex). Both rayMul and rayDiv round up (add HALF_RAY), so scaledBorrow.rayMul(index).rayDiv(index) can equal scaledBorrow + 1. When attempting to repay the full group debt, erc20DecreaseCrossBorrow computes amountScaled = debtAmount.rayDiv(index) which may exceed the actual userScaledCrossBorrow by 1, causing arithmetic underflow revert. This makes specific positions unliquidatable.
threats.investigation_rationale: If liquidation reverts for certain positions due to rounding, bad debt accumulates. An attacker could deliberately create positions with scaled amounts that trigger this edge case, making their positions immune to liquidation during price drops.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20DecreaseCrossBorrow: amountScaled = amount.rayDiv(groupData.borrowIndex); then groupData.userScaledCrossBorrow[account] -= amountScaled which can underflow when amountScaled > actual scaled balance due to rounding

preconditions: User has a scaled borrow balance where scaledBorrow.rayMul(index).rayDiv(index) == scaledBorrow + 1
Full debt repayment is attempted (e.g., during liquidation or user repay)
Specific scaled amount and index values that trigger the rounding edge case

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rayMul rounds up: (a * b + HALF_RAY) / RAY; rayDiv rounds up: (a * RAY + b/2) / b; combined: identity + 1 in edge cases
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker creates borrows with specific amounts targeting the rounding edge case, making full repayment/liquidation revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker creates unliquidatable position, borrows against it, and defaults without consequence

economic_questions: What is the probability of hitting the rounding edge case for random amounts?
Can an attacker reliably craft amounts that trigger this?
Does the -1 adjustment in curDebtRepayAmount > remainDebtToLiquidate path in _repayUserERC20Debt mitigate this?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test: find (scaledAmount, index) pairs where rayMul then rayDiv overshoots
Test full debt repayment at those values to confirm underflow revert
Check if liquidation path handles edge case differently from user repay path

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full borrow amount becomes bad debt if position unliquidatable
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Position created in one tx; exploited over time as price moves

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Create position, then wait for collateral price decline
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol accumulates bad debt from unliquidatable positions

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders of the borrowed asset

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current borrowIndex values for deployed assets
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Systematic fuzz testing of rayMul/rayDiv identity property

threats.title: Isolate Auction First Bid Minimum Equals Borrow Amount Creating Permanent Bad Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: The first bid in an isolate auction must be >= vars.borrowAmount AND >= vars.liquidatePrice. If the NFT floor price drops significantly below the accumulated debt (borrowAmount >> NFT value), no rational bidder will bid because they'd pay more than the NFT is worth. The loan becomes permanently stuck: can't be liquidated (no bidder), can't be repaid by owner (they abandoned it), creating unrecoverable bad debt. calculateNftLoanLiquidatePrice confirms the liquidatePrice is bounded by borrowAmount from below.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike cross-margin liquidation where collateral is ERC20 (always has some liquidation-worthy value), NFT collateral can become worthless while debt accumulates. The auction mechanism has no fallback for worthless collateral, leading to systemic bad debt accumulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(params.amounts[vars.nidx] >= vars.borrowAmount) enforces bid >= debt; when debt >> NFT value, no rational bidder participates

preconditions: NFT floor price drops significantly (e.g., >50%)
Accumulated debt with interest exceeds NFT market value
No bidder willing to take a guaranteed loss on the auction

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: First bid requires bidAmount >= borrowAmount, but borrowAmount can exceed NFT value. liquidatePrice = max(nftPrice * (1 - bonus), borrowAmount) so liquidatePrice >= borrowAmount.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not active manipulation; systemic risk from market conditions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit; bad debt locks lender funds permanently

economic_questions: What percentage of historical NFT price drops would trigger this scenario?
Is there a governance mechanism to write off bad debt?
Can the protocol socialize the loss or force liquidation at market price?
validation_requirements: Simulate NFT price drop: verify no rational bid is possible
Check if admin can intervene to close stuck positions
Quantify potential bad debt accumulation under various market scenarios

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - market condition issue, not active attack

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Lender funds locked; cannot be withdrawn; supplier bank run risk

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders of the borrowed asset
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to bad debt vs total pool liquidity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all underwater isolate loan debt

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Historical NFT price volatility vs configured LTV/liquidation thresholds (6000-8000 bp)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model bad debt accumulation under various drawdown scenarios

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC20 Collateral Bonus Calculation Allows Liquidator Over-Extraction via Oracle Timing
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calculateAvailableERC20CollateralToLiquidate, the liquidation bonus is applied as percentMul(PERCENTAGE_FACTOR + liquidationBonus). When collateral price is stale-high while debt price updates to current, the liquidator captures bonus on the inflated collateral price. The function computes baseCollateral using current oracle prices, then applies the bonus. If collateral oracle lags behind debt oracle, the maxCollateralToLiquidate is inflated, allowing the liquidator to seize more collateral than market conditions warrant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol uses oracle prices without staleness checks (confirmed in Phase A). Different assets may have different oracle update frequencies, creating windows where relative prices are stale. Liquidators who observe this can time their liquidation calls to extract maximum bonus.
deployment_assumptions: Oracle update frequencies differ between collateral and debt assets
No explicit staleness check in IPriceOracleGetter implementation
threats.config_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol configures Chainlink aggregators for WETH, USDT, DAI with different aggregator addresses that may have different heartbeat intervals

proof_of_signal.pattern: baseCollateral = (debtToCover * debtAssetPrice * collateralAssetUnit) / (debtAssetUnit * collateralPrice); maxCollateralToLiquidate = baseCollateral.percentMul(PERCENTAGE_FACTOR + liquidationBonus); no staleness validation on either price

preconditions: Collateral oracle price is stale-high (hasn't updated after price drop)
Debt oracle price is current
Borrower's position is liquidatable based on mixed stale/current prices
Liquidation bonus is configured (500-1000 bp from deployment)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: collateralPrice from oracle used directly; if stale-high by X%, maxCollateralToLiquidate inflated by X%
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Liquidator monitors oracle update delays and triggers liquidation during optimal price staleness window
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extra collateral seized = (stalePrice - currentPrice) / currentPrice * liquidatedAmount

economic_questions: What are the actual heartbeat intervals for configured Chainlink feeds?
What is the maximum price divergence within a heartbeat window?
Does the PriceOracle implementation add any staleness checks?
validation_requirements: Check PriceOracle.sol for staleness validation (not in provided code)
Analyze historical Chainlink update frequencies for configured feeds
Calculate maximum extractable bonus from typical staleness windows

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Additional 1-5% on top of normal liquidation bonus during price movement
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Moderate; depends on oracle staleness window

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient debt tokens to execute liquidation
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During rapid price movements when oracle update lags
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Borrowers lose more collateral than fair-value liquidation warrants

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Borrowers being liquidated during stale oracle windows
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Additional 1-5% collateral loss beyond intended bonus
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All liquidatable positions during price volatility events

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Chainlink aggregator heartbeat intervals; configured liquidationBonus values
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate liquidation with stale vs current prices

threats.title: SupplyLogic.executeDepositERC20 Supply Cap Check Uses Stale Index Before Update
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeDepositERC20, InterestLogic.updateInterestIndexs is called BEFORE validateDepositERC20. However, validateDepositERC20's supply cap check computes totalSupplyWithFee as (totalScaledCrossSupply + accruedFee).rayMul(assetData.supplyIndex). Since updateInterestIndexs has already updated the index AND accrued fees, the accruedFee may be larger than expected. More critically, if the index update in the same block was already done by another operation, the accruedFee accumulated from a PREVIOUS block's update plus the current one could push the supply cap check to reject valid deposits, or the rounding from index multiplication could create a tiny gap allowing deposits above the intended cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: Supply caps are a critical safety parameter. If the cap check uses slightly inaccurate values due to index timing, deposits near the cap boundary could behave unexpectedly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Supply cap check: totalSupplyWithFee = (totalScaledSupply + assetData.accruedFee).rayMul(assetData.supplyIndex); accruedFee is accumulated scaled amount that when rayMul'd with a growing index could create boundary imprecision

preconditions: Supply cap configured for the asset (supplyCap != 0)
Total supply is near the cap boundary
AccruedFee has accumulated significantly since last collection

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalSupplyWithFee includes accruedFee which grows between fee collections; large accumulated fees could block deposits even when actual supply is below cap
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not directly exploitable for profit but can deny service to depositors
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; potential griefing by preventing deposits into nearly-full pools

economic_questions: How large can accruedFee grow between collections?
Does the accruedFee correctly represent claims against available liquidity?
Can an attacker force deposits to fail by manipulating accruedFee growth?
validation_requirements: Simulate deposit near supply cap with large accumulated fees
Verify cap check accuracy vs actual underlying supply
Test edge cases with fee collection timing

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When accumulated fees are large and supply near cap
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Depositors blocked from supplying; potential yield loss

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model accruedFee growth rate vs supply cap headroom

threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Fails to Clear Locker Address, Permanently Bricking Re-Deposited NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: When an isolate loan is liquidated, the protocol fails to reset the NFT's `lockerAddr`. If the liquidated NFT is ever re-deposited into the protocol, it becomes permanently stuck and cannot be withdrawn.
threats.investigation_rationale: `executeIsolateLiquidate` deletes `loanData` and clears the owner, but does not reset `tokenData.lockerAddr` to `address(0)`. If the NFT is re-deposited, `validateWithdrawERC721` will revert because it strictly requires `lockerAddr == address(0)` for all withdrawals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Clears owner and supplyMode but omits tokenData.lockerAddr = address(0)

preconditions: An NFT is liquidated via isolate margin.
The same NFT is later re-deposited into the protocol.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incomplete state cleanup during liquidation leaves residual lock flags.

operational_questions: Can the locker address be manually cleared by an admin?
Does this affect cross-margin deposits of the same NFT?
validation_requirements: Liquidate an isolate loan, re-deposit the NFT, and attempt to withdraw it.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an active attack, but a severe operational failure.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Liquidator receives NFT and sells it on the open market.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Unsuspecting user buys the NFT and deposits it into BendDAO V2.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User attempts to withdraw the NFT and transaction permanently reverts.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of user assets (NFTs stuck in protocol).
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to clear the locker addresses.

threats.title: Missing Oracle Price Validation Allows Zero-Price Mass Liquidations
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol does not validate oracle prices for zero values. If an oracle returns 0 for a collateral asset, the protocol calculates the user's collateral value as 0 without reverting, immediately making the position liquidatable.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `GenericLogic.calculateUserAccountData`, `assetPrice` is multiplied by the balance. If `assetPrice == 0`, `userBalanceInBaseCurrency` becomes 0. This drops the health factor below 1.0. A temporary oracle failure or manipulation returning 0 can trigger unwarranted mass liquidations.
deployment_assumptions: Oracle can return 0 during extreme market events or failures.
threats.config_evidence: DeployPoolFull.s.sol configures PriceOracle, which relies on external Chainlink aggregators that may return 0 or stale data if unvalidated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check for vars.assetPrice == 0 before using it in multiplication.

preconditions: Oracle returns 0 for a collateral asset.
Liquidator bots are monitoring the mempool.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Implicit trust in oracle data without standard sanity checks (e.g., > 0, staleness).

operational_questions: Does the `PriceOracle` contract implement zero checks internally?
Can an attacker force the oracle to return 0 via flash loans?
validation_requirements: Review `PriceOracle.sol` (not provided) for zero checks. If absent, simulate a 0 price return and trigger liquidation.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Oracle experiences a failure or is manipulated to return 0 for Asset X.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All users using Asset X as collateral instantly have a Health Factor < 1.0.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker seizes collateral at a massive discount.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Mass unwarranted liquidations; severe loss of user funds.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very High - irreversible loss of user collateral.

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - relies on external oracle state.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sudden spikes in liquidations across multiple accounts.

threats.title: Proxy forwarding/dispatch may allow msgSender spoofing via trailing calldata if trusted-sender validation is missing or misconfigured
threats.hypothesis_description: All user-facing modules derive msgSender from the last 20 bytes of calldata (unpackTrailingParamMsgSender). If the core dispatcher/dispatch() pathway fails to enforce that only protocol proxies append this trailing sender, an attacker can directly call module functions and craft calldata that sets msgSender to any victim address. This would bypass ValidateLogic.validateSenderApproved checks (which compare msgSender vs onBehalf) and enable unauthorized withdrawals/borrows/supply mode changes on behalf of victims.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a system-wide identity boundary. A single missing check in the unseen dispatch layer turns all per-user authorization into attacker-controlled input, enabling direct asset theft across all pools.
deployment_assumptions: The actual deployed call path allows direct calls to module contracts OR dispatch() does not strictly enforce trustedSenders/proxy provenance
trustedSenders mapping is not correctly populated for all forwarding proxies
threats.config_evidence: No dispatch()/trustedSenders enforcement code is included in provided contracts; BaseModule relies on unpackTrailingParamMsgSender() regardless.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msgSender := shr(96, calldataload(sub(calldatasize(), 40))) // trusts trailing calldata

preconditions: Attacker can reach module functions without an authenticated forwarder appending the real sender
Or dispatch() accepts attacker-controlled trailing calldata as authoritative sender
Victim has supplied assets or approvals such that operations are meaningful (e.g., withdrawable balance)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Identity is taken from calldata rather than msg.sender; security depends on external dispatch/trustedSenders correctness
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Yes (correct module/proxy installation and trustedSenders wiring)

operational_questions: Can EOAs call module proxies directly and choose trailing msgSender?
Does PoolManager.dispatch validate msg.sender against trustedSenders and enforce correct appended sender?
Are module proxies and trustedSenders populated for every moduleId, including single-proxy vs multi-proxy modules?
validation_requirements: Review PoolManager.dispatch implementation and trustedSenders checks
On a fork, attempt direct call to BVault.withdrawERC20 with forged trailing sender and observe whether it succeeds
Verify InstallModule/DeployPoolFull wiring populates trustedSenders correctly for all module proxies

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Craft calldata for a module function with victim address in last 20 bytes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call module/proxy/dispatch entry directly (whichever is permissive)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute unauthorized withdraw/borrow to attacker-controlled receiver

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full effective per-user authorization bypass (takeover of user accounts)
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Immediate TVL drain; loss of trust; emergency shutdown likely
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very high (would require pausing/upgrade and compensating users)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All user operations relying on msgSender extraction

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low if dispatch check is missing; otherwise not exploitable

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transactions where msg.sender is not a known proxy but msgSender (decoded) varies
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Withdraws/borrows attributed to addresses that did not sign/call tx origin
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Burst unauthorized operations across many users

threats.title: Untrusted ERC20 or ERC721 token behavior can lock the protocol due to strict balance-delta invariants on transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: VaultLogic enforces exact balance deltas for ERC20 transfers in/out and between wallets (poolSizeAfter == poolSizeBefore ± amount). Tokens with transfer fees, rebasing, blocklists, or non-standard balance semantics can cause reverts and lock deposits/withdrawals/repays/liquidations for that asset. While the documentation claims support for weird ERC20 traits, these strict checks explicitly reject them, creating an operational DoS vector if such assets are listed.
threats.investigation_rationale: Asset listing is an operational action; if a non-standard token is configured (intentionally or accidentally), core flows revert and can trap collateral/debt positions, causing losses and governance incidents.
deployment_assumptions: An asset with fee-on-transfer, rebasing, or restrictive transfer rules is added/activated in a pool
Users have positions in that asset when the behavior changes (e.g., token turns on fees/blacklists)
threats.config_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol lists WETH/USDT/DAI (standard). Docs state 'weird traits' are in scope, indicating likely future listing risk; no on-chain guardrails shown to prevent listing such tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(poolSizeAfter == (poolSizeBefore + amount), Errors.INVALID_TRANSFER_AMOUNT)

preconditions: A configured ERC20 exhibits balance changes not equal to transferred amount (fee-on-transfer, rebasing, deflationary, etc.) OR blocks transfers for some users
Users attempt affected operation paths (deposit/withdraw/repay/liquidation/auction redeem fine transfer)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Exact balance delta checks contradict stated support for weird token traits
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Yes (asset onboarding and continued token behavior)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After asset listing or after token changes behavior

operational_questions: Does protocol intend to support fee-on-transfer/rebasing tokens, or should onboarding prevent them?
