Can a token change behavior post-listing (toggle transfer fee/blacklist) and freeze the market?
Is there an emergency offboarding/migration path when transfer checks start failing?
validation_requirements: Test with fee-on-transfer and rebasing mock tokens across deposit/withdraw/repay/liquidate/auction flows
Review configurator/onboarding (unseen) for any token-behavior allowlist/denylist checks
Assess emergency procedures to pause/remove asset and unwind positions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Get a problematic token listed (or wait for listed token to toggle restrictions)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users create positions; then token behavior triggers reverts on exits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Positions become stuck; liquidations/repays fail; solvency degrades
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker profits via systemic chaos (e.g., shorting, capturing other liquidations) - off-protocol
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires token listing or token issuer action

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: market freezes for the asset; users stuck; potential cascading insolvency if debt cannot be repaid
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High (may require upgrade and/or token-specific handling)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ERC20 flows touching affected asset, including auction escrow movements

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Most damaging during drawdowns when exits/liquidations are critical

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverts with INVALID_TRANSFER_AMOUNT clustered by asset
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden halt in withdrawals/repays for a specific token
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Token issuer toggling fees/blacklists coincident with failures

threats.title: Proxy fallback can emit arbitrary logs when called by creator, enabling event forgery/confusing operational monitoring
threats.hypothesis_description: Proxy.fallback contains a special branch: if msg.sender == creator, it does not forward calls but instead interprets calldata as log topics/data and emits LOG0-LOG4. This allows the creator contract to emit arbitrary events from the Proxy address, potentially spoofing operational signals (e.g., fake deposits/borrows) relied upon by indexers, monitors, or accounting systems.
threats.investigation_rationale: While it requires creator involvement (usually the core PoolManager), it is a powerful operational footgun: any bug/compromise of creator can forge logs at proxy addresses. This is especially impactful in modular systems where proxies may be treated as authoritative event emitters.
deployment_assumptions: Off-chain systems (indexers/risk monitors/accounting) treat events emitted from Proxy addresses as authoritative without cross-checking state
Creator contract has a code path (or can be induced) to call Proxy directly with crafted calldata
threats.config_evidence: Proxy.sol explicitly implements creator-only log emission in fallback; no deployment scripts show off-chain monitoring assumptions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: If caller==creator: calldatacopy + log0..log4 with attacker-controlled topics/data

preconditions: Creator (core contract) can call Proxy with arbitrary calldata (either by design or due to another bug)
Operational monitoring relies on Proxy-emitted events for alerts/accounting without validating state diffs

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Log emission is decoupled from state transitions; logs can be manufactured.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Creator contract control required (trusted role / compromise).

operational_questions: Do modules intentionally emit events via proxies using this mechanism? If so, are events always coupled to state changes elsewhere?
Do any off-chain systems accept proxy events without verifying state?
Can a compromised creator forge liquidations/borrows to trigger off-chain actions?
validation_requirements: Review creator/dispatcher code paths for any ability to call proxies in the creator context
Assess indexer/monitor pipelines for event-only assumptions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Gain ability for creator to execute a crafted call to Proxy (via bug or privileged misuse)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Emit events mimicking high-severity protocol actions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit downstream automation/monitoring confusion (alerts, accounting, liquidator bots)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: False alarms, accounting errors, automated bot misbehavior
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium—requires monitoring hardening and incident response

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Proxy-emitted events with no corresponding state changes in PoolStorage
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Discrepancies between indexed events and on-chain balances
